message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-10 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, on-box data-sovereignty, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use python/pytest/gh as needed.

FINDING SEC-10-1 (high, SEC): libpq PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE env vars bypass the loopback guard on the Postgres checkpointer (graph state leaves the box)
Location: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:157-164 (get_postgres_connection_string) → :275 (ConnectionPool(conn_str,...)); guard src/assay_engine/_local.py:110 (require_local_uri)
Claim: require_local_uri validates only the connection STRING. libpq merges connection parameters from the environment (PGHOSTADDR, PGHOST, PGPORT, PGSERVICE+PGSERVICEFILE) for any parameter not explicitly present in the string. The default DSN 'postgresql://localhost:5432/assay' has an explicit host but NO hostaddr, so libpq uses the env var PGHOSTADDR as the actual TCP target while 'host=localhost' is used only for TLS/SNI. Setting PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 (or PGSERVICE pointing at a service file with a remote host, or PGHOST when the DSN form 'dbname=x' — which the guard accepts — carries no host) makes psycopg/libpq connect off-box even though require_local_uri passed. Confirmed: psycopg.conninfo.conninfo_to_dict('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay') yields {'dbname','host','port'} with no 'hostaddr', so PGHOSTADDR is merged at connect time.
Why real: This defeats the module's own stated invariant ('graph state must not leave the box', checkpoint.py:161) under standard, documented libpq environment-variable semantics — no malicious server or exotic input required, just an env var an operator/CI/upstream process can set. The guard re-audit explicitly targets 'remaining bypass' cases; this is a concrete data-egress path the string-only check cannot see. No code anywhere scrubs or pins PG* env vars (grep for PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGSERVICE in src returns nothing outside comments).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
140	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
141	    not pass as ``conn_str``.
142	
143	    Precision caveat (pass 5, #H-023): layer 1 is an EXACT-substring replacement, so it scrubs the
144	    primary connection string precisely only when that string appears verbatim in ``text``. If a
145	    backend reformats it (re-quoting, reordering DSN keywords, percent-encoding the password) the
146	    verbatim match misses and only the layers-2–3 regex backstops apply — which still strip
147	    ``://[REDACTED]@`` userinfo and ``password=[REDACTED] tokens, but are not guaranteed exhaustive for
148	    every backend message shape. It is "precise when the conn string appears verbatim", not
149	    "always precise".
150	    """
151	    if conn_str:
152	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
153	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
154	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
155	
156	
157	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
158	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
159	
160	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
161	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
162	    """
163	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
164	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
165	
166	
167	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
168	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
169	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
170	    that can raise)."""
171	    try:
172	        pool.close()
173	    except Exception:
174	        pass
175	
176	
177	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
178	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
179	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
180	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
181	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
182	    with _init_lock:
183	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
184	    for p in pools:
185	        _safe_close(p)
186	
187	
188	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
189	    global _atexit_registered
190	    with _init_lock:
191	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
192	        if not _atexit_registered:
193	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
194	            _atexit_registered = True
195	
196	
197	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
198	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
199	    with _init_lock:
200	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
201	        if lock is None:
202	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
203	        return lock
204	
205	
206	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
207	    with _init_lock:
208	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
209	
210	
211	def _acquire_migration_lock(conn: Any) -> None:
212	    """Acquire the cross-process schema-migration advisory lock with a bounded wait (#129).
213	
214	    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
215	    so a stuck/crashed initializer cannot hang bootstrap indefinitely; the resulting error
216	    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
217	    (not ``SET``) so the value can be safely parameterized.
218	    """
219	    timeout_ms = _migration_lock_timeout_ms()
220	    if timeout_ms:
221	        conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", (str(timeout_ms),))
222	    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
223	
224	
225	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
226	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
227	
228	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
229	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
230	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
231	    or the extra is not installed.
232	    """
233	    try:
234	        if use_memory:
235	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
236	
237	            return MemorySaver()
238	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
239	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
240	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
241	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
242	        raise RuntimeError(
243	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
244	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
245	        ) from exc
246	
247	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
248	
249	    # Fast path: reuse a live, self-healing pool for this conn_str rather than opening a new pool
250	    # (and its background worker thread) on every call (#144). The liveness check on checkout
251	    # replaces dead connections, so a cached pool stays valid across transient outages.
252	    cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)
253	    if cached is not None:
254	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
255	
256	    # Serialize bootstrap PER conn_str — NOT process-globally (#129). Unrelated databases
257	    # initialize concurrently, and a stuck advisory lock on one conn_str can never block init of
258	    # another. The brief meta-lock (_init_lock) is only taken inside the helpers, never held
259	    # across the blocking advisory-lock wait / DDL below.
260	    with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):
261	        cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)  # another thread may have built it while we waited
262	        if cached is not None:
263	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
264	
265	        failure: str | None = None
266	        pool: Any = None
267	        try:
268	            # Pool bounds are env-tunable (#F-040): the reasoning seam already runs 8 worker
269	            # threads, so a fixed max_size=8 can be saturated under concurrent reasoning+checkpoint
270	            # load on larger hardware. connect_timeout bounds an individual TCP connect so a
271	            # black-holed host fails fast instead of hanging bootstrap for the OS default.
272	            min_size = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1"))
273	            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8"))
274	            connect_timeout = max(1, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT", "10"))
275	            pool = ConnectionPool(
276	                conn_str,
277	                min_size=min_size,
278	                max_size=max_size,
279	                kwargs={
280	                    "autocommit": True,
281	                    "prepare_threshold": 0,
282	                    "row_factory": dict_row,
283	                    "connect_timeout": connect_timeout,
284	                },
285	                check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
286	                open=True,
287	            )
288	            _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
289	            # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
290	            # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
291	            # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
292	            with pool.connection() as conn:
293	                _acquire_migration_lock(conn)  # bounded wait so a stuck peer can't hang us (#129)
294	                try:
295	                    # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
296	                    # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
297	                    # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
298	                    PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
299	                finally:

1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def is_local_socket_path(host: str | None) -> bool:
42	    """True for a libpq Unix-domain-socket host — a leading-'/' absolute directory (#K-SEC-1).
43	
44	    libpq accepts ``host=/var/run/postgresql`` (or the query form ``?host=/tmp``) to connect over
45	    a local Unix-domain socket — a connection that NEVER touches the network, so it is strictly
46	    more data-sovereign than the TCP loopback the guard already permits. A single leading '/' is a
47	    local path; a Windows UNC prefix (two leading separators, e.g. ``//server`` or ``/\\server``)
48	    resolves to a remote SMB share and must still be rejected.
49	    """
50	    if not host:
51	        return False
52	    h = host.strip()
53	    # A comma means libpq MULTI-HOST (e.g. "/tmp,evil.com") — never a single socket dir. Reject so
54	    # the leading-'/' check can't wave through an off-box host hidden after the comma (#K-SEC-9-1).
55	    # Callers split multi-host and validate each element; a leaf reaching here must be one host.
56	    if "," in h:
57	        return False
58	    return h.startswith("/") and not h.startswith("//") and not h.startswith("/\\")
59	
60	
61	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
62	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
63	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
64	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
65	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
66	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
67	            f"from {url!r}"
68	        )
69	    return url
70	
71	
72	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
73	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
74	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
75	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
76	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
77	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
78	        )
79	    return host
80	
81	
82	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
83	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
84	
85	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
86	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
87	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
88	
89	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
90	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
91	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
92	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
93	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
94	    """
95	    if "@" in netloc:
96	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
97	    hosts: list[str] = []
98	    for part in netloc.split(","):
99	        p = part.strip()
100	        if not p:
101	            continue
102	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
103	            end = p.find("]")
104	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
105	        else:
106	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
107	    return hosts
108	
109	
110	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
111	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
112	
113	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
114	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
115	    reports, since the two can disagree:
116	
117	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
118	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
119	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
120	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
121	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
122	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
123	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
124	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
125	
126	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
127	    """
128	    try:
129	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
130	        netloc = parsed.netloc
131	        query = parsed.query
132	    except ValueError:
133	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
134	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
135	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
136	        ) from None
137	
138	    if netloc:
139	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
140	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
141	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
142	        if "\\" in netloc:
143	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
144	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
145	            )
146	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
147	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
148	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
149	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
150	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
151	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
152	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
153	                )
154	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
155	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
156	                # libpq allows a COMMA-SEPARATED multi-host value; split and validate EVERY element
157	                # (#K-SEC-9-1) — else "/tmp,evil.com" slipped through because the whole string
158	                # looked like a socket path and the off-box host after the comma was never checked.
159	                for h in (p.strip() for p in val.split(",")):
160	                    if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
161	                        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
162	                            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
163	                            f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
164	                        )
165	        return uri
166	
167	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
168	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
169	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
170	                raw = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
171	                # Split libpq's comma-separated multi-host and validate each element (#K-SEC-9-1).
172	                for h in (p.strip() for p in raw.split(",")):
173	                    if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
174	                        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
175	                            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
176	                            f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
177	                        )
178	        return uri
179	
180	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
181	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
182	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
183	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
184	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
185	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
186	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
187	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
188	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
189	        )
190	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
191

content.text:
The finding claims `require_local_uri` validates only the connection string and can't see PG* env vars. That much is technically true about the function. The key question is whether libpq actually merges PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE when the DSN has an explicit `host=localhost`, and whether there's any mitigation elsewhere.

Let me check the claim about libpq env var precedence and whether any env scrubbing exists.

input.description: Search for PG env var handling and conninfo usage

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python -c "import psycopg; print('psycopg', psycopg.__version__)" 2>&1; python -c "from psycopg.conninfo import conninfo_to_dict; print(conninfo_to_dict('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay'))" 2>&1
input.description: Check psycopg available and conninfo parse

content.text: Now the critical test: does libpq actually use PGHOSTADDR when `host=localhost` is explicit in the DSN but `hostaddr` is not? Let me verify with the actual libpq via psycopg's expand step.

input.description: Inspect libpq effective host/hostaddr params with PGHOSTADDR set

content.content:
b'service' val= None envvar= b'PGSERVICE' compiled= None
b'host' val= b'localhost' envvar= b'PGHOST' compiled= None
b'hostaddr' val= None envvar= b'PGHOSTADDR' compiled= None
b'port' val= b'5432' envvar= b'PGPORT' compiled= b'5432'

content.text:
`Conninfo.parse` only parses the string, it does not merge env. The real merge happens at connection time via libpq's `PQconnectdb`. The key question: when `host=localhost` is present in the conninfo but `hostaddr` is absent, does libpq read `PGHOSTADDR` from the environment?

Per libpq semantics, an environment variable is used as the default **only if the parameter is not specified directly in the conninfo string**. `hostaddr` is NOT specified in the DSN, so `PGHOSTADDR` would be consulted. Let me confirm this is real behavior by actually attempting a connection and observing where it tries to connect.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 PGCONNECT_TIMEOUT=2 .venv/bin/python -c "
import psycopg, time
t=time.time()
try:
    psycopg.connect('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay', connect_timeout=2)
except Exception as e:
    print('elapsed', round(time.time()-t,2))
    print(type(e).__name__, str(e)[:300])
"

input.description: Compare connection behavior with and without PGHOSTADDR

content.content:
=== CONTROL: no PGHOSTADDR (should hit localhost:5432) ===

=== TEST: PGHOSTADDR=127.0.0.1 but port to a closed local port via DSN, vs remote ===
--- PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 (TEST-NET-3, non-routable -> timeout, NOT refused) ---
elapsed 3.05 | ConnectionTimeout connection timeout expired

content.text:
Interesting and decisive. The **control** (no PGHOSTADDR, host=localhost:5432) produced **no output / no error** — meaning it either connected or failed instantly (connection refused on localhost since no PG running). But the **PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5** case **timed out after 3s** — proving libpq actually attempted a TCP connection to the remote non-routable address `203.0.113.5`, not to localhost.

Let me confirm the control's behavior to nail this down (refused vs connected) and rule out that PGHOSTADDR also affected the control.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== CONTROL host=localhost, no env (expect fast 'refused' since no PG) ===" && env -u PGHOSTADDR -u PGHOST -u PGSERVICE PGCONNECT_TIMEOUT=3 .venv/bin/python -c "
import psycopg,time
t=time.time()
try:
    c=psycopg.connect('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay', connect_timeout=3); print('CONNECTED?!')
except Exception as e: print('elapsed',round(time.time()-t,2),'|',type(e).__name__,'|', str(e)[:200])
"
echo
echo "=== Confirm: same DSN, PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 => target is REMOTE (3s timeout) ==="
echo "Proven above. Now PGHOSTADDR=192.0.2.1 (also non-routable) sanity:"
env -u PGHOST -u PGSERVICE PGHOSTADDR=192.0.2.1 PGCONNECT_TIMEOUT=2 .venv/bin/python -c "
import psycopg,time
t=time.time()
try: psycopg.connect('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay', connect_timeout=2)
except Exception as e: print('elapsed',round(time.time()-t,2),'|',type(e).__name__)
"
input.description: Confirm control fails fast (refused) vs remote timeout

content.content:
=== CONTROL host=localhost, no env (expect fast 'refused' since no PG) ===
CONNECTED?!

=== Confirm: same DSN, PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 => target is REMOTE (3s timeout) ===
Proven above. Now PGHOSTADDR=192.0.2.1 (also non-routable) sanity:
elapsed 2.04 | ConnectionTimeout

content.text:
Decisive confirmation:

- **Control** (no PGHOSTADDR, `host=localhost:5432`): `CONNECTED?!` — there IS a local Postgres, libpq connected to localhost instantly.
- **PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5** and **PGHOSTADDR=192.0.2.1**: the connection no longer reaches the local server; it **times out attempting the remote non-routable address**. libpq used `PGHOSTADDR` as the actual TCP target even though the DSN's `host=localhost` passed the guard.

This empirically proves the finding's core claim: `require_local_uri` passes `postgresql://localhost:5432/assay`, but libpq merges `PGHOSTADDR` (absent from the string) and connects off-box. `host=localhost` is then used only for TLS/SNI, not the connection target. The guard's stated invariant ("graph state must not leave the box") is defeated by a standard documented libpq env var.

Let me verify there's no env-scrubbing at process startup or in the pool kwargs that I might have missed, and check whether the codebase pins `hostaddr`/clears PG* anywhere.

content.text:
The tests confirm the team already knows libpq dials `hostaddr` (test line 110: "libpq dials hostaddr") and explicitly guards against `hostaddr` *in the string*. But there is **no** test or code path that scrubs/pins the `PGHOSTADDR` **environment variable**. The default DSN carries no `hostaddr`, so the env var wins at connect time.

I have empirically confirmed the finding holds. The verdict is STANDS.

input.reasoning:
I attempted to refute SEC-10-1 but could not. The finding holds and is a real production data-egress defect.

Empirical proof (psycopg 3.3.4 + libpq, run in the project venv against the default DSN postgresql://localhost:5432/assay):
- psycopg.conninfo.conninfo_to_dict('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay') => {'dbname','host','port'} with NO 'hostaddr' key, exactly as the finding claims. require_local_uri (src/assay_engine/_local.py:110) only inspects the string's authority hosts and any host/hostaddr query/DSN tokens; it never reads the environment.
- Control connect (env -u PGHOSTADDR, host=localhost:5432): libpq connected to the local Postgres instantly ("CONNECTED?!"), proving a live local server.
- With PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 (and again 192.0.2.1, both non-routable TEST-NET): the SAME DSN no longer reaches the local server — it times out after the full connect_timeout (2-3s) attempting the REMOTE address. This proves libpq used PGHOSTADDR as the actual TCP target while host=localhost was reduced to a TLS/SNI label, i.e. the connection left the box despite require_local_uri passing.

No mitigation exists: grep over src/ finds zero handling of PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE/PGPORT (no os.environ.pop/del, no hostaddr pinning in _DEFAULT_PG or the ConnectionPool kwargs at checkpoint.py:275). The team is aware libpq dials hostaddr — tests/test_persistence.py:110 even comments "libpq dials hostaddr" — but only guards hostaddr present IN THE STRING, not the env var that supplies it when absent. The pool then passes the bare conn_str to ConnectionPool(conn_str, ...), so libpq performs the env merge unguarded.

Out-of-scope rebuttals do not apply: this defeats the module's own explicitly stated invariant ("graph state must not leave the box", checkpoint.py:161 / ADR-0003 data sovereignty), requires no malicious server or exotic input — just a standard documented libpq env var an operator/CI/parent process can set — and the guard re-audit explicitly targets remaining string-bypass cases. This is a concrete, real, env-driven off-box egress path the string-only check structurally cannot see.

Severity high (data sovereignty / state egress) is appropriate. Files: src/assay_engine/_local.py:110-165 (string-only guard), src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:157-164 and :275 (default DSN with no hostaddr passed to ConnectionPool).

A correct fix would pin connect-controlling env-merge parameters in the resolved conninfo (e.g. set hostaddr explicitly when host is loopback, or clear/validate PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE before connecting), not merely validate the string.

content.text:
Verified empirically. The finding STANDS — I could not refute it.

The core claim is real and reproducible against the actual code with psycopg 3.3.4: setting `PGHOSTADDR` to a remote address makes libpq connect off-box even though `require_local_uri('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay')` passes, because the default DSN carries no `hostaddr` token and the string-only guard never inspects the environment. The control (no env var) connected to local Postgres; PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 / 192.0.2.1 timed out attempting the remote target, proving libpq merged the env var as the actual TCP destination. No env scrubbing or `hostaddr` pinning exists anywhere in `src/`.
